actions.thought: Holding A6s on the button in a 3-handed game is a strong opening candidate. I will raise to 3x the big blind to seize the initiative and potentially take down the blinds or play a pot in position with a hand that has good post-flop equity.

I am in the Small Blind with 6s 2d, which is an extremely weak hand. Facing a raise from the Button, calling is a losing play as I will be out of position with a weak range, and there is no reason to 3-bet. The most disciplined and mathematically sound decision is to fold and preserve my stack for a better spot.

I am in the Big Blind with Q3o, facing a 3x raise from the Button. Q3o is a very weak and disconnected starting hand. Playing it out of position against a pre-flop raiser is highly unprofitable. Folding is the standard and correct play.

Jack-Five offsuit is a very weak hand with poor connectivity and low board coverage. Even in the button position, there is no strategic value in calling a 100-chip bet with such a marginal holding. It is optimal to fold and preserve my stack for a better spot.

Action folded to me in the Small Blind and I have Ace-Ten offsuit. This hand is well ahead of the Big Blind's random defending range. Limping would allow the Big Blind to realize equity for free with their weaker hands. Raising to 300 (3x the big blind) is a standard open from this position to extract value and potentially win the pot outright pre-flop.

I am in the Big Blind with 8-5 suited. The Small Blind has raised to 300, which is a standard 3x open. I need to call 200 more to see a flop in a pot that will be 600. The odds are 3:1, which is generous for a hand with decent post-flop playability and straight/flush potential. Against a typical Small Blind opening range, 8-5 suited is a standard defense. I will call to see the flop.

I have middle pair with top kicker (Ace-Ten) on a Q-T-6 board that features a flush draw. Being out of position against the Big Blind, this is a marginal made hand best played for pot control. Checking allows me to keep the pot manageable, protects my checking range, and gives me the flexibility to bluff-catch or see a free turn card.

I have 8-high on a board of Ts-6s-Qd. I have some very marginal backdoor straight potential (5-6-8 needs a 7 and then a 4 or 9). The pre-flop aggressor (Pro) checked. While I could attempt a bluff, this board hits a standard Small Blind opening range quite well (containing many Queens and Tens). Checking back allows me to see a free turn card and potentially improve or find a better bluffing opportunity later if Pro continues to show weakness.

The turn brings the 9h, completing some straight draws but keeping the board very wet with flush and straight possibilities. Since Flash checked back the flop, his range is likely capped, mostly consisting of marginal pairs, floats, or draws. My middle pair with top kicker (Ace-Ten) is likely the best hand right now. It is crucial to bet here to deny free equity and extract value from worse hands like flush draws, straight draws, or newly paired 9s. I will bet half the pot to charge his draws and protect my hand.

On the turn, the board is Ts-6s-Qd-9h. I am holding 8c-5c, which is just 8-high with a gutshot straight draw (needing a 7 to make a 6-10 straight). Pro, who raised pre-flop and checked the flop, has now bet 300 into a 600 pot. While the pot odds are 3:1, my hand has very little equity against a range that includes Queens, Tens, or even Nines. Calling for a 4-outer is mathematically incorrect, and I have no showdown value to justify a call or a bluff-raise on this coordinated board.

In a 3-handed game, the button opening range is quite wide. Q6 suited is well within a standard opening range. I will make a 2.5x raise to 250 to try to steal the blinds or play a pot in position with suited cards.

Ace-Ten offsuit is a strong hand in a 3-handed game, especially when facing a standard button open which typically represents a wide range of hands. I will 3-bet to 850 to take the initiative and put pressure on the button, while also making it expensive for the big blind to enter the pot. This size is standard for being out of position.

I hold Jack-deuce suited in the big blind. The action consists of a button open and a 3-bet from the small blind. This is a very strong range, and my hand is far too weak to defend against a 3-bet, especially while being out of position. Folding is the only mathematically sound play here to preserve my stack.

I opened Qd6d on the button as a marginal steal. The Small Blind (Flash) 3-bet to 850, a significant sizing. Q6s plays poorly against a 3-betting range, being dominated by better Queens, better flush draws, and stronger pairs. Calling here is unprofitable over the long run, and folding is the only standard play.

Ten-Five offsuit is a very weak hand, even in a three-handed game from the button. It lacks connectivity and high-card strength, making it difficult to play post-flop if called. Given the early stage of the tournament and the lack of specific reads on the blinds, there is no reason to risk chips trying to steal with such a low-equity hand. Folding is the standard and most disciplined play here.

My hand, Ten-Six suited, is relatively weak and playing out of position against the Big Blind is a disadvantage. With a stack of 98 big blinds, there is no need to contest marginal pots. Folding is the disciplined choice to conserve my stack for stronger starting hands.

My hole cards are 7-2 offsuit, which is statistically the worst starting hand in No-Limit Hold'em. As I am in the button position, there is no incentive to enter the pot with such a weak hand against two opponents. Folding is the optimal strategic decision to preserve my stack for more favorable opportunities.

With an offsuit one-gapper (87o) in the Small Blind against the Big Blind, the hand is generally too weak to play out of position. Folding is the most straightforward, GTO-approved action to avoid bleeding chips in marginal spots.
I am on the button with 95 offsuit, which is a very weak hand with extremely low equity against any calling range. The standard play is to fold and wait for a better spot to attack the blinds.

I am in the Small Blind with K4o. The Button (Pro) has folded, leaving only the Big Blind (Flashlight) to act after me. While K4o is a marginal hand, in a heads-up situation from the blinds, it is strong enough to play. Raising here allows me to potentially take down the blinds immediately or take control of the hand. I will raise to 300, which is a standard 3x open.

I am in the Big Blind with 8h 6h. The Small Blind raised to 300, and I need to call 200 to see the flop in a pot that will be 600. Suited connectors like 8h 6h have good playability and equity against a standard Small Blind opening range. Given the pot odds of 3:1, calling is a standard defense to prevent the opponent from stealing the blinds too easily.

On a flop of 2d-4s-3d, I have top pair (4c) with a King kicker. The board is quite coordinated, featuring both a straight draw (any Ace or 5) and a diamond flush draw. Since I was the pre-flop aggressor and have top pair, I should lead out with a continuation bet to extract value from draws and Ace-high hands, while also protecting my hand from being outdrawn on the turn. A bet of 300 (half the pot) is a standard sizing to accomplish this.

The board texture 2d-4s-3d is highly coordinated and heavily favors the Small Blind's opening range, which likely includes sets (22, 33, 44) and straights (A5). My hand, 8h 6h, has completely missed this flop, offering no pair and no legitimate straight or flush draws. With no equity to justify a call, folding is the disciplined play to preserve my stack for a more favorable opportunity.

94o is a very weak hand even for a 3-handed button open. Given my aggressive image from previous hands, I expect the blinds might start playing back at me soon. Folding is the disciplined choice here as there is no need to splash around with bottom-tier trash.

I am in the small blind heads-up against the big blind after the button folded. With K-7 offsuit, my hand is too marginal to raise effectively, but calling to see a flop is a low-risk way to play out of position given the pot odds.

I am in the Big Blind with 6c 2c and the Small Blind limped. This hand is too weak to isolate with a raise, but since there is no raise to call, I can happily check and see a free flop in position post-flop.

I am out of position with King-high and a gutshot straight draw (needing a six). The board is highly coordinated, hitting the big blind's defending range hard. Betting here would be a bluff without much equity or fold equity against a calling range, so checking is the standard play to control the pot size.

With a 6-high flush draw on a coordinated board, I have decent equity but little showdown value. Flashlight's check signals weakness. Betting half pot (100 chips) serves as a semi-bluff to win the pot immediately, while still having outs to improve if called.

Facing a bet of 100 into a 150 pot, I hold King-high with a gutshot straight draw (requiring a 6). My equity is approximately 8.5% (4 outs), which is well below the required pot odds of roughly 28%. Given the coordinated nature of the board and the unlikelihood of my King-high being the best hand, calling would be mathematically negative EV. Therefore, folding is the correct, disciplined play.

I am in the button position with Queen-Nine suited, which is a strong enough hand to open from this position. By raising to 250, I can take the initiative, put pressure on the blinds, and potentially take down the pot pre-flop or play in position post-flop.

I am in the Small Blind facing a Button raise. My hand is 8s 6d, which is an extremely weak, offsuit, disconnected hand. Playing this out of position against a raise is a significantly losing proposition, so the standard and optimal play is to fold.

I am holding 7c 2h, which is widely considered the weakest starting hand in Texas Hold'em. Flashlight opened from the button for 2.5x. While the pot odds are 2.6:1, defending 72o out of position is a losing play in the long run as it rarely flops well and is difficult to play post-flop. I will fold and wait for a better spot.
97s is a solid speculative hand to play from the Button. Raising allows me to steal the blinds or take the initiative into the flop while maintaining positional advantage.

I am in the small blind with T8o. The button (Pro) has opened for a standard 2.5x raise to 250. While ranges in 3-handed play are wider, T8o is a relatively weak hand to play out of position. Calling would require me to put in another 200 chips to see a flop where I will be first to act, which is generally unprofitable with marginal connectors. Folding allows me to wait for a better spot with more equity or positional advantage.

I am holding Seven-deuce suited in the big blind, which is one of the weakest hands in Texas Hold'em. Despite getting decent pot odds to call the 150 chip raise, the lack of playability and the disadvantage of being out of position against an aggressive button raiser makes calling a negative expected value (-EV) play. Folding is the disciplined choice to preserve my stack.
I am on the button with A3o. In a 3-handed game, this is a standard opening hand. I'll raise to 2.5x the big blind (250) to apply pressure on the blinds and take control of the pot.

I am in the small blind with a very weak hand, 4c6s. Playing out of position against an opening raise from the button is highly disadvantageous and negative EV. I should conserve my stack and fold to wait for a better spot.

Ten-Six offsuit is a very weak hand. Defending the big blind against a button raise with such a disconnected and offsuit garbage hand is a long-term losing play. We fold and wait for a better spot to invest our chips.

I am on the button with a pocket pair of 6s, which is a strong starting hand in a 3-handed game. I will open-raise to 250 to take the initiative and potentially win the pot pre-flop or position myself for a strong post-flop play.

I am in the Small Blind with 9To, an offsuit connector. Flashlight raised from the Button. 9To is too weak to play out of position against a button raise, especially with the Big Blind still left to act behind me. Folding is the standard and safest play.

I am in the Big Blind with 94o, which is a very weak hand. Flashlight opened from the Button to 250. While the pot odds to call are roughly 2.6:1, 94o plays very poorly out of position and is frequently dominated or misses the flop entirely. Given the tournament context and the weakness of my hand, folding is the most prudent play to conserve my stack.
I am dealt Q7o on the button. This is a very weak offsuit hand, well outside any standard opening range. Folding is the correct decision here.

I am in the Small Blind with 8c 2c. After the Button (Pro) folds, the action is on me. While 82 suited has a small amount of playability, it is generally considered a trash hand. In a Blind vs Blind situation, I could potentially complete the bet or raise to steal, but playing out of position with such a weak hand is usually negative expected value. I will choose the safest route and fold to conserve my stack for better opportunities.

On the button in a 3-handed game, I have a wide opening range to take advantage of position. 4c 6c is a suited hand with some connectivity that plays well post-flop if called. I've shown aggression in previous hands with similar sizing (300), so I'll maintain that consistency to apply pressure on the blinds and potentially take the pot down pre-flop or play with initiative in position.

I am in the small blind with Jack-high, offsuit, unsuited, and disconnected. This is a weak hand to defend with, especially out of position against a button raise. Playing a pot out of position with a marginal hand like 7c-Jh is negative expected value. Folding is the disciplined, standard play.

Q6o is a very weak hand to defend from the Big Blind against a 3x open from the Button. Playing out of position with a marginal, uncoordinated offsuit hand is unprofitable. I will fold and wait for better starting cards.

7h 9d is a weak, unsuited hand. Even on the button, there is no strategic value in playing this hand against two opponents who have shown aggression in previous hands. Folding is the disciplined play to preserve my stack for stronger holdings.

I am in the Small Blind with Ten-Six offsuit, which is a very weak starting hand. With the Button folding, I am facing the Big Blind out of position. Given the poor playability and lack of equity of T6o, the most profitable long-term decision is to fold rather than limp or raise.

King-Jack offsuit is a strong broadway hand, especially on the button in a 3-handed game. By raising to 2.5x the big blind, I can take the initiative, potentially win the blinds uncontested, or play a heads-up pot in position against either of the blinds if they decide to call.

I am holding T3 offsuit in the Small Blind. Pro has opened with a raise from the Button. T3o is a very weak hand that plays poorly out of position, especially against a standard opening range. Calling 200 into a 400 pot gives me okay odds, but the high likelihood of being dominated and the disadvantage of being out of position for the rest of the hand makes this a clear fold.

I am in the Big Blind with 8-4 offsuit, which is a very weak hand. The pot is 400, and I need to call 150 to see the flop. Given the strength of my hand and the fact that I will be out of position for the rest of the hand, calling is mathematically incorrect. It is better to preserve my stack and wait for a more favorable opportunity.

I am on the button with Jd 5d, a suited hand that plays reasonably well in position. The table has been somewhat folding to button raises so far. I will put in a standard opening raise of 2.5x to 250 to take control of the pot and pressure the blinds.

I am in the small blind with 9c 6d, which is a weak hand with poor playability, especially out of position against a button raise. The hand lacks sufficient equity to justify calling, so I will fold to preserve my stack.

With T8s in the Big Blind, we are facing a standard Button open to 2.5x. Suited one-gappers play reasonably well post-flop, and we only need to call 150 chips to win a total pot of 400. Defending the Big Blind here is a standard and profitable play against a likely wide Button range.

On this paired flop of 9d Qd Qs, I have ten-eight of clubs, giving me a gutshot straight draw to a jack. As the pre-flop caller out of position, the standard play is to check to the pre-flop aggressor. Leading out (donk betting) here is not ideal as the board favors the raiser's range, which contains many Queens, overpairs, and better broadway cards. I will check to see what Flash does.

I have a flush draw on a paired board. I was the pre-flop aggressor, and after the Big Blind checked to me, a continuation bet is standard here to apply pressure and potentially win the pot immediately. My hand has decent equity even if called, and this board texture favors my range as the button raiser.

We hold T8 suited with a gutshot straight draw on a paired Q-Q-9 board with two diamonds. Our draw is very weak, lacking any backdoor flush equity, and even if we hit a Jack, we could run into a full house or a higher straight. Folding to a half-pot continuation bet here is the standard and most disciplined play.

Holding 9h-7h on the button is a strong position to play. Suited connectors have good board coverage and play well post-flop. Given I have not been active recently, opening the pot with a raise allows me to take initiative and potentially steal the blinds or see a flop in a favorable position. A raise to 250 is a standard open size.
Holding an offsuit connector (67o) in the Small Blind against a Button raise is a standard fold. The hand lacks suitedness and high-card value, and we would be playing out of position post-flop.

I am in the big blind with 3s 4c, which is a very weak hand. Flashlight raised from the button to 250. Although I am getting decent pot odds to call (150 to call into a pot of 400), 34o is near the bottom of starting hands and plays very poorly out of position. Given my chip lead, there is no reason to defend such a weak range against a button open. I will fold and wait for a better spot.
Holding an offsuit 6-7 on the Button. This is a very weak and speculative hand, making it a clear fold pre-flop. I'll save my chips for better starting hands.

I am in the Small Blind with K7o. Since the Button has folded, it is just me and the Big Blind. K7o is a strong enough hand to raise for value and to attempt to take down the blinds immediately. My standard open size has been 3x the big blind, so I will raise to 300 to apply pressure on the Big Blind.

My hand, five-trey offsuit, is one of the weakest possible starting hands. Defending against a raise from the small blind while I am in the big blind with such a marginal holding is statistically unprofitable, particularly since I would be playing out of position post-flop. Folding is the most disciplined choice to conserve my stack for better playing opportunities.

I am on the button with Q3 offsuit. While I have been aggressive in opening from late positions in previous hands, Q3o is significantly below a standard opening range even for a three-handed game. My stack is healthy, and I've successfully stolen several pots recently. To maintain a credible image and avoid over-committing with marginal cards that have poor post-flop equity, I will fold this hand and wait for a better spot.

I am in the small blind with Ace-Deuce offsuit. Since the button folded, I am heads-up against the big blind. With an Ace in hand, calling the small blind to see a flop is a solid, standard play to maintain a reasonable pot size while exercising position post-flop, as it allows me to realize my equity and potentially outplay the big blind if the board texture is favorable.

T9o is a marginal hand. Since the small blind has only limped, checking allows me to see a free flop in position. This is a low-variance play that realizes the equity of my hand without building a pot unnecessarily with mediocre holdings.

I have bottom pair on a relatively dry board. As the small blind acting first, checking is the standard approach to control the pot size and see how my opponent reacts. Given the low strength of my pair and being out of position, I do not want to overcommit to the pot at this stage.

We flopped top pair with a ten kicker on a 9-high board that contains a flush draw (two hearts) and some straight draws. Our opponent checked, showing weakness, but might be holding a draw or a weaker pair. Betting here protects our equity against overcards and draws while extracting value from worse made hands like a pair of fours, deuces, or A-high. A half-pot bet of 100 is appropriately sized to accomplish both value and protection goals.

With bottom pair and an Ace kicker, I have a decent bluff-catcher against the big blind, who could be betting with a variety of hands including draws or weaker pairs. The pot odds are 2:1 (calling 100 to win a 200 pot), which makes calling a statistically sound decision to see the turn. Folding would be too conservative given the board texture, and raising would be overplaying a marginal hand.

I have improved to two pair (nines and twos) on the turn. While this is a strong hand, it remains vulnerable to higher pairs or better trip combinations. Acting first out of position, checking is the prudent choice to control the pot size and evaluate my opponent's action, allowing me to respond accordingly rather than committing chips to a bloated pot with a medium-strength hand.

The turn brings a second 9, giving us trip nines. With the 9c and 9d on the board and the 9s in our hand, the opponent holding the only remaining 9 (9h) is extremely unlikely. Flashlight's flop call indicates they likely have a heart flush draw, a straight draw (like A3 or A5), or a weak made hand such as a pair of 4s or a low pocket pair (55-88). We need to extract value and charge these draws a premium to see the river. Betting around 60% of the pot builds the pot with our extremely strong hand while likely keeping those weaker holdings and draws in the hand.

I have two pair (nines and twos) with an Ace kicker. While my hand has improved, it remains vulnerable to any nine (trips) or better pocket pairs, which are within the opponent's range given their aggression on the flop and turn. However, the pot odds of approximately 2.6 to 1 justify a call to see the river, especially since I have an Ace kicker and could potentially be ahead of a missed heart draw or a smaller pocket pair. Folding would be premature, and raising against a betting opponent into a 650 pot is too risky.

On the river, I have a marginal two pair (nines and twos) which functions effectively as a bluff-catcher rather than a value-betting hand. Given the opponent's sustained aggression on the flop and turn, they have polarized their range toward either a strong made hand or a complete bluff. Betting into the opponent would be counterproductive, as it would likely fold out their bluffs while getting called by hands that have me beat. Checking is the optimal strategy to control the pot size and allow the opponent an opportunity to bluff or check back, enabling me to reach a showdown cheaply.

On the river, the flush draws and most straight draws have bricked. We hold trip nines, which is exceptionally strong on this board. Flashlight's check-calls on the flop and turn suggest they might be holding a medium pocket pair (like 55-88), a smaller pair like 4x or 6x, or a missed draw. Since missed draws will fold regardless, we need to size our bet to extract maximum value from marginal made hands. A half-pot bet of 450 is a solid size that can induce a crying call from stubborn pocket pairs or bluff-catchers.

I am facing a bet of 450 into a pot that has grown to 800, creating a total pot of 1250. My hand, two pair (Nines and Twos with an Ace kicker), is a classic bluff-catcher on this board. While any pocket pair or a Nine beats me, the opponent's aggressive line could represent a missed heart flush draw or a lower-value bluff. Given the pot odds, I only need to be correct approximately 27% of the time to make this a profitable call. With the Ace of hearts in my hand, I block some combinations of flush draws, but calling remains the standard mathematical play to prevent being pushed off my equity by potential bluffs.

I am on the button with 9c 6c. This is a speculative but playable hand in late position. By opening with a raise, I can attempt to steal the blinds or take initiative in the hand, putting pressure on the small and big blinds. A sizing of 2.5x the big blind (250) is standard for this spot to balance opening frequency and risk.

I am dealt an extremely weak and unsuited hand (4h 7d) in the small blind. Facing an open-raise from the button, there is no justification to put any chips into the pot. Folding is the only viable option.

I have Ace-Jack suited, which is a premium hand in a 3-handed game. Flashlight raised from the button, which often indicates a wide range. I want to build the pot and take control of the hand. 3-betting to 850 is a standard value raise from the Big Blind to put pressure on the button opener.

I opened the button with 9c 6c, which is a standard steal attempt. Flash (Big Blind) has 3-bet to 850, significantly increasing the cost of the hand. 96s is a speculative hand that plays poorly against a 3-bet range, as it lacks high card strength and is dominated by the hands that typically 3-bet from the blinds. Calling to see a flop would commit more chips to a bloated pot where I would be out of position relative to the aggressor's perceived strength, and 4-betting as a bluff with this hand is too risky given the stack sizes and frequency of 3-bets. Folding is the most profitable long-term decision here to preserve my stack.

I am on the button with 2d 6s, which is an extremely weak, unsuited, disconnected starting hand. Even though I am on the button, this hand has practically zero playability or showdown value, so folding pre-flop is the correct and standard play.

I am in the Small Blind with King-Tray offsuit. The Button has folded, making this a blind-vs-blind situation. Based on previous hands like #13 and #18, raising to 300 from the Small Blind has been effective in taking the pot pre-flop against Flashlight. K3o is a decent hand to continue this aggressive blind-stealing strategy.

Ace-Jack offsuit is a premium hand in a blind-versus-blind scenario. Flash has been playing aggressively, raising frequently from the small blind. By 3-betting to 900, I can effectively exploit this aggression, take initiative, and potentially win the pot pre-flop or set up a favorable continuation bet on most flops. This also puts pressure on Flash's wider range, allowing me to leverage my positional advantage.

I raised from the Small Blind with King-Trey offsuit, but Flashlight responded with a 3-bet to 900. K3o is a weak hand that does not play well against a re-raise, and calling 600 more would put me in a difficult spot out of position post-flop. Given the strength shown by the Big Blind, the most disciplined play is to fold and wait for a better opportunity.

I am on the button with Jack-Deuce of spades. While J2s is suited, it is a very weak hand even in a three-handed game. I have a healthy stack and have been active in previous hands. Given the recent aggression from Flashlight and Pro, opening with such a marginal hand from the button invites a 3-bet that I would be forced to fold to. It is better to wait for a stronger range to continue my aggression.

I am in the small blind with a premium starting hand (Ace-King offsuit) after the button folded. This is a prime opportunity to play heads-up against the big blind. I should raise to isolate and take control of the pot, aiming to win the blinds or build a larger pot if I get a call. A raise to 300 is standard sizing for this situation.
Facing a raise from the small blind, my hand (Jack-Deuce offsuit) is far too weak to defend the big blind. Folding is the standard and correct play.

Qs-4s is a very weak hand, even when in position on the button. Since there is no prior action to justify playing a marginal hand and the potential to be re-raised by the blinds is high, folding is the most disciplined and long-term profitable decision.

Folded to me in the Small Blind. I have K3 suited. This is a decent hand to attempt a blind steal with, while retaining some playability post-flop if called. I'll open with a standard 3x raise to 300 to put pressure on the Big Blind.

I am in the Big Blind holding 7-2 offsuit, which is the weakest starting hand in poker. Pro raised from the Small Blind to 300. While I am getting 3-to-1 odds to call, this hand has extremely poor playability post-flop and is not worth defending against a raise from a competent opponent. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

J5 suited is a very weak hand even on the button. While stealing the blinds is important in short-handed play, J5s lacks playability post-flop and doesn't connect well enough to justify an open raise. The best and lowest variance play here is to fold and wait for a better starting hand.

I am in the small blind with 4h 2d, which is an extremely weak hand. Although it is only 50 chips to call the big blind after the button folded, 42o has very poor playability and is likely dominated. In a 3-handed game, I should maintain a solid range, and folding this trash hand is the correct professional move.

I am on the button with Jack-Deuce offsuit. While it is a 3-handed game and button opening ranges should be wide, J2o is significantly below the threshold of a profitable opening hand even for a steal attempt. Flashlight (SB) and Pro (BB) have shown they can be active or defensive. Folding here is the standard and most disciplined play to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

My hand, 4h-7d, is very weak and lacks potential. Being in the small blind puts me out of position for the rest of the hand. Given the tournament stage and the strength of my hand, it is mathematically better to fold and preserve my stack for a more advantageous situation rather than chasing with poor cards.

6-3 suited is a weak starting hand in a 3-handed game, even with the advantage of being on the button. My stack is currently the shortest at the table, making it important to play conservatively and avoid unnecessary chip loss with speculative hands. Folding is the most disciplined decision to preserve my stack for a more favorable opportunity.

With the action folded to me in the Small Blind, K7o is a reasonable hand to attempt a steal. It has blockers to premium King hands and can often win the pot uncontested if the Big Blind has a marginal or weak hand. I'll raise to 250 to apply pressure.

I am in the Big Blind with King-Eight offsuit (Ks 8h). The Small Blind (Pro) has raised to 2.5x (250 total). In a blind-versus-blind scenario, my hand is well above the average defending range. Since I will have position on the Pro for the rest of the hand and the pot odds are favorable (150 to call into a pot of 350), calling is the most standard and effective play to realize equity.

We raised pre-flop with K7o as a steal from the Small Blind, and the Big Blind called. The flop of Qd Th 5c completely missed our hand, giving us only King high with no realistic draws. Because this board connects heavily with the Big Blind's pre-flop calling range (broadway hands, medium pairs, etc.), a continuation bet is likely to be called. The most prudent play here is to check and re-evaluate, with the intention of folding to a bet.

I have King-high on a board of Queen-Ten-Five. The Small Blind raised pre-flop and has now checked to me. This board likely connects with a standard Small Blind opening range, but the check suggests either a mediocre hand or a complete miss. While I could bet to try and take the pot immediately, King-high has some showdown value against his total misses, and checking back allows me to see a free turn card in position without inflating the pot with a weak hand.

I improved to bottom pair with the 7 on the turn. Since Flash checked behind on the flop, his range is likely capped at marginal pairs or high-card hands. Checking allows me to control the pot size with a marginal made hand, potentially induce a bluff from missed draws or high cards, and evaluate his action. Betting would mostly fold out hands I beat while keeping hands that beat me (like Qx or Tx).

I have King-high in a blind-versus-blind pot that has been checked by the Small Blind on both the flop and the turn. The board (Q-T-5-7) is relatively dry. While I could bet to try and take down the pot, my King-high has a fair amount of showdown value against the Small Blind's air/missed hands in this specific configuration. By checking back, I see a free river card and keep the pot small, which is a prudent way to play a hand with marginal value in position.

While Flash has shown aggression in previous hands, 9-4 offsuit is not a hand I want to play out of position against a range that includes raises. The potential for being dominated is too high, and there is no reason to put more chips into the pot with this holding.

I am on the button with a pocket pair of Sevens (7d 7c) in a 3-handed game. This is a strong starting hand that should be played aggressively from the button to either take down the blinds or maintain initiative post-flop. A standard 3x opening raise is appropriate here.

Qc Jc is a strong suited connector. Being in the Small Blind against a Button raise puts me at a positional disadvantage. A 3-bet is an effective way to neutralize the button's positional advantage, seize the initiative, and potentially take down the pot pre-flop. Given the stack depths, a raise to 950 is a standard size that allows me to represent strength and put pressure on the opener.
I am in the Big Blind with Jack-Ten offsuit. Facing an open raise from the Button and a 3-bet from the Small Blind, my hand is far too weak to continue. This is an easy fold.

I opened with Pocket Sevens on the button and faced a 3-bet from the Small Blind. In a 3-handed game, 77 is a very strong hand to defend, especially when I have position throughout the rest of the hand. While the 3-bet size is significant, folding would be overly cautious. Calling allows me to see a flop, evaluate the texture, and potentially set-mine or win the pot post-flop with a positional advantage.

The flop is extremely unfavorable for my hand (Qc Jc). The Ace and King on the board are perfect cards for the button's calling range, as they likely contain many broadway cards, Aces, and Kings. I have a gutshot straight draw (needing a Ten) and a backdoor flush draw, but I am currently way behind. Betting into this board would be a mistake as it is unlikely to fold out a better hand, and I would be bloating the pot while out of position against a range that crushes this board. Checking is the most prudent play to maintain pot control and re-evaluate on the turn.

The flop of Ad-Kd-3c is extremely favorable for Flashlight's 3-betting range (which likely includes AA, KK, AK, AQ). My pocket sevens are now essentially a bluff catcher. Flashlight's check could be a trap with a monster or a sign of weakness with a hand like JJ or QQ that is afraid of the Ace and King. Since I have some showdown value against a total whiff and very little equity if I bet and get called, checking back is the most prudent play. It keeps the pot manageable and allows me to see the turn for free to evaluate Flashlight's next move.

The turn card (3d) pairs the board, which is an interesting development. Both players checked the flop, indicating a lack of strong Aces or Kings in both ranges. Since I have absolutely no showdown value with Queen high, betting could be a candidate for a bluff, but the pairing of the 3 makes it more likely my opponent has a piece of the board or a pocket pair that won't fold. Checking is the most disciplined approach to maintain pot control and re-evaluate the river, as I have no significant draws that warrant a semi-bluff on this specific texture.

Flashlight has checked for the second time on a board that heavily favors a 3-betting range (containing both an Ace and a King). My pocket sevens have very little value here; I am behind any Ace, King, or pocket pair higher than sevens. While Flashlight's checks could indicate a 'give-up' with a hand like Queens or Jacks, or even a total whiff, betting is unlikely to force a fold from a better hand and will certainly not be called by a worse one. By checking back, I control the size of the pot and take a free card to the river, hoping to either hit a seven or reach a cheap showdown.

The board is Ad 3c Kd 3d Ah. Both players have checked the flop and turn, which suggests that neither player connected strongly with the Ace and King high board, or both are playing cautiously. My Queen high is essentially air. Betting here would be a pure bluff, attempting to push the opponent off a lower pair or total air. However, with two Aces on the board, it is difficult to represent that I have one. Given the likelihood that the opponent could check-call with a small pocket pair or even a King, checking is the most logical and safe play to conclude the hand without bloating the pot unnecessarily on a bluff that is unlikely to succeed.

The board is Ad Ah 3c 3d Kd, which means the best five-card hand I can make is the board itself (Two Pair: Aces and Threes with a King kicker), as my pocket Sevens are lower than the King. Flashlight 3-bet pre-flop and has checked every street. In this situation, I am either splitting the pot with any hand that doesn't contain an Ace, King, or Trey (like QQ, JJ, or air), or I am losing to a hand that does. Betting serves no purpose as it won't fold out a better hand and won't be called by a worse one. Checking to see the showdown is the only correct play.

Ten-Eight suited is a strong playable hand in late position. As the button, I have the positional advantage and should look to take the initiative. A standard opening raise to 300 is appropriate to potentially win the blinds or set up a favorable post-flop scenario.

K3 offsuit is a very weak hand to play from the small blind facing a raise from the button. Playing this hand out of position will likely lead to difficult spots post-flop and negative expected value. Folding is the standard and most profitable decision here.

I am holding 6c 7c in the Big Blind. Flashlight, the Button, has raised to 300. The Small Blind has folded. I need to call 200 more into a pot of 450, giving me 2.25:1 odds. 6-7 suited is a strong speculative hand that plays well post-flop, and given the pot odds and the fact that I am closing the pre-flop action, a call is the standard and most profitable play here.

I have middle pair (sevens) on a Jack-high board (J-7-3 rainbow). I am out of position against the pre-flop aggressor (Flashlight). The standard play here is to check to the raiser to see their continuation bet sizing and frequency. Donk-betting here would likely only be called by better hands or draws that have good equity against me, so checking is the most balanced and informative play.

I am on the button with T-8 suited on a 3-7-J rainbow flop. Having raised pre-flop, my opponent checked to me. My hand has no pairs or strong draws, only overcard potential. In a three-handed game, checking back allows me to maintain pot control and see a free card without bloating the pot with a weak hand. This keeps my range disguised and avoids being check-raised by a stronger J or pocket pair, preserving my stack for better opportunities.

I have second pair (7s) on a J-7-3-9 board. Flashlight (the pre-flop aggressor) checked back on the flop, which often signals a weak hand or a hand with some showdown value that doesn't want to build a large pot. The 9s turn adds some straight possibilities (T8) and a spade draw. I will check again to control the pot size and see if Flashlight bets. If he checks again, I likely have the best hand and can look for a thin value bet or a check-call on the river. Checking is a safe way to proceed with a medium-strength hand out of position.

The turn card, 9s, has significantly improved my hand to a massive combo draw: I now have an open-ended straight draw (7-8-9-10-J) and a flush draw with my two spades. With such high equity against a checking opponent, I should take the lead to semi-bluff and build a larger pot. Betting 450 into the 650 pot puts maximum pressure on the opponent and ensures I am well-positioned if I improve my hand on the river.

I have second pair (sevens) on a board of J-7-3-9. Flashlight raised pre-flop but checked back on the flop, which often indicates a capped range or a lack of a strong top pair (Jack). The nine on the turn is a potential semi-bluff card or a card that improved a marginal hand. Given the pot odds (calling 450 to win a total pot of 1550) and my middle pair, I have sufficient equity to call and see if my hand holds up or improves on the river.

On the river, the board is J-7-3-9-2. I hold 6c 7c, giving me a pair of sevens (third pair). Flashlight raised pre-flop, checked the flop, and bet the turn after I checked. This sequence often indicates a delayed continuation bet or a hand that improved on the turn, like a nine or a straight draw that turned into a pair. However, checking the flop also caps his range, making it less likely he has a premium hand like AJ or a set. My pair of sevens has some showdown value, but it is not strong enough to bet for value. I will check to see if Flashlight wants to check behind for a showdown or if he attempts a bluff, at which point I will evaluate based on his bet sizing.

I have flopped a flush (Ten-high) on the river, as the board shows three spades and I hold the Ten and Eight of spades. The opponent checked to me on the river. Given the strength of my hand, I should go for value. While it is not the absolute nuts (higher flushes are possible), a flush is a very strong holding. Betting 750 into the 1550 pot allows me to get value from hands like a straight, two-pair, or trips that may have called the turn bet, without over-committing if the opponent happens to be trapping with a higher flush.

On the river, the board is J-7-3-9-2 with three spades. I hold 6c 7c for a pair of sevens (third pair). Flashlight raised pre-flop and checked the flop, which typically caps his range and makes a strong Jack unlikely. However, he bet the turn (9s) and has now bet the river (2s). The turn and river cards are both spades, completing any spade flush draws he might have semi-bluffed on the turn. Additionally, any Nine (which he could have turned) or a straight like T8 (which he could have had) now beats me. While I am getting roughly 3:1 pot odds, my hand is very weak on this specific runout, and Flashlight's aggression on the turn and river after a flop check-back suggests he either improved to a better hand or is representing the flush. Given the board texture and the action, I do not have enough equity to justify a call with third pair.

K4o is a very weak and marginal hand, even on the Button in a 3-handed game. Opening with it risks facing a 3-bet from the aggressive players in the blinds or playing a dominated King post-flop. The best play is to fold and wait for a stronger starting hand.

I am in the small blind with 3-2 offsuit, which is the weakest possible starting hand in poker. Although the button folded, attempting a steal from the small blind with this hand is not advisable against an active big blind. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

I am on the button with 72 suited. While it's a 3-handed game and I could theoretically attempt to steal the blinds, 72 is the weakest hand in poker. I have a healthy stack and there's no reason to take a high-variance risk with such a poor starting hand at this stage of the tournament. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am in the Small Blind and the Button has folded, leaving me heads-up against the Big Blind. My hand, King-Five offsuit, is marginal, so calling the additional 50 chips to see a flop is a standard play to realize my equity and leverage my position.

I have K6o in the Big Blind after the Small Blind just limped. It's a marginal hand that isn't strong enough to consistently raise for value or as a bluff, but getting to see a free flop in position is a profitable outcome. I will check and play post-flop.

I am in the Small Blind, first to act on the flop with King-Five offsuit. The board of Queen-Ten-Eight is connected and hits the likely range of the Big Blind (Pro) much harder than my range. Since I have completely missed the flop with no significant draw or pair, attempting a bluff here is high-risk and low-reward. Checking is the most prudent play to see if the turn card improves my hand or to minimize losses if Pro decides to bet.

The flop of Qd Ts 8d connects heavily with a typical Small Blind limping range, which includes many broadways, suited connectors, and diamond draws. I completely missed the board with K6o and have no equity besides King-high. Betting here as a bluff is unlikely to succeed given how wet the board is, so checking behind to take a free turn is the optimal play.

The board of Queen-Ten-Eight-Nine is highly connected and hits the likely range of the Big Blind (Pro) effectively. Holding King-Five offsuit with no pair and no legitimate straight or flush draw, I have zero equity to continue betting. Since the opponent checked the flop, they may be trapping or have a marginal hand that they are unwilling to commit with yet. Checking here is the standard play to maintain pot control and minimize losses, as any bet I make will likely be a pure bluff that is unlikely to force a fold on this coordinated board.

With King-high and no meaningful draw on a highly connected board (Q-T-9-8), betting as a bluff is very risky since any piece of the board or a Jack/Seven easily calls or raises. The opponent has checked twice, so checking behind to see a free river is the most prudent and safe play.

The river card four of hearts does not improve my King-high hand. The opponent has checked throughout, suggesting either a very weak hand or a trap. Betting now is unlikely to generate folds from any hand that beats me and risks unnecessary chips. Checking is the most logical path to showdown.

We have reached the river in a limped pot with King-high. The opponent has checked all three streets on a highly connected Q-T-9-8-4 board. While we could attempt to bluff to fold out Ace-high or bottom pair, the pot is small and it's not worth risking chips when our King-high occasionally wins at showdown against missed diamond or straight draws. Checking behind is the safest and most standard play.

I am on the button with Q6s, a hand with decent equity and playability in position. Given that I am in the late position, opening the pot is a standard strategic move to potentially steal the blinds or take initiative in the hand. A raise to 300 is a consistent sizing that I have used previously to apply pressure to the blinds.

I am dealt 8-2 offsuit in the Small Blind, which is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. The Button has opened with a raise to 3 big blinds. Given my extremely poor hand strength and positional disadvantage, folding is the only profitable long-term decision.

I am in the Big Blind with A5o. Flashlight raised to 300 from the Button, and the Small Blind folded. I need to call 200 more into a pot of 450, giving me pot odds of 3.25:1. On a flop of Tc-5d-2c, I have middle pair with an Ace kicker. Being out of position against the pre-flop raiser (Flashlight), the standard play is to check to the aggressor. If Flashlight bets, my middle pair is strong enough to call at least one street in a 3-handed game, especially since I block some Ace-high hands and have a solid kicker.

I hold a Queen-high flush draw on a board of 2c 5d Tc. As the pre-flop aggressor, checking is a missed opportunity. Betting here maintains my initiative and puts pressure on the opponent. I have middle pair (5s) with the best possible kicker (Ace) on a relatively low board of Tc-5d-2c. Flashlight's button-opening range is wide, and their continuation bet of 350 into 650 is standard. Folding would be too weak given my equity against a range that includes overcards, flush draws, and gutshots. Raising is unnecessary as it might fold out bluffs and isolate me against better hands like a Ten or an overpair. Calling allows me to keep the pot manageable and see how the turn develops while keeping Flashlight's bluffs in.

On the turn, the King of Spades appears as an overcard to my middle pair. The King is a card that likely connects well with a Button opening range (Kx hands like KQ, KJ, KT). By checking, I can see how Flashlight responds to this scare card. If they check back, I can cheaply see the river; if they bet, I will re-evaluate based on the sizing, though my hand's relative strength has decreased now that it's third pair.

I hold the Queen-high flush draw with the Qc on a board of 2c 5d Tc Ks. My opponent has checked to me on the turn. In position, taking a free card is a high-value play that allows me to realize my equity without bloating the pot with a drawing hand. Betting now would likely only be called by hands that currently beat me or by draws I don't necessarily want to push off immediately. Checking back allows me to maintain control of the pot size and decide on the river based on the card that falls.

On the river, the board is Tc-5d-2c-Ks-5c. I have improved to three-of-a-kind (trip fives). Since the river 5c completed the board, I have trips with the best possible kicker (Ace). While I could bet to get value from a King or a Ten, checking allows me to induce a bluff from a missed draw (like a straight draw or club draw) or get value from a smaller bet Flashlight might make with a King. Given the previous action and my position, a check-raise or a check-call is a solid way to maximize value against a wide range.

I have rivered a flush with [Qc, 6c] on a board of [2c, 5d, Tc, Ks, 5c]. My opponent has checked to me on the river. Given the strength of my hand and the fact that I am in position, checking behind would be a significant missed value opportunity. I should place a value bet to extract chips from any pairs or weaker hands that may have called my previous actions, or simply to get paid off by a range that includes other clubs or strong kings. A bet of 700 represents roughly half the pot, which is a solid sizing to induce a call.

On the river board of 2c-5d-Tc-Ks-5c, I have trip fives with the best possible kicker (Ace). The turn went check-check, suggesting Flashlight likely has a marginal made hand (like a Ten or a King) or a draw. The 5c on the river is an excellent card for me. Flashlight has now bet 700 into a pot of 1350 after I checked. Given the action, Flashlight could be value betting a King or a Ten, or potentially bluffing with missed straight draws (like 43s or 64s) or missed club draws. While a raise might get value from a King, it also risks folding out bluffs or getting called/re-raised by a full house (like TT or 22, though less likely given the turn check). Calling is a very safe and high-frequency play here to ensure I win the pot against his bluffs and value bets, especially since the club flush also completed on the river. However, trip fives with an Ace kicker is exceptionally strong in a 3-handed game, and I believe I can get called by a King if I raise small, but calling is the most disciplined way to capture the current bet and avoid being blown off the hand by an unexpected monster.

Q7o is a relatively weak offsuit hand. Even on the button, it falls outside the standard opening range against active blinds in a full or 3-handed game. I will fold to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

I am in the Small Blind with 9-high offsuit (9h 6c). The Button (Pro) has folded. Playing a weak hand like 96o out of position against a sticky opponent is generally a losing strategy in the long run. Therefore, I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am on the button with 7-4 offsuit, which is a very weak hand. While it's a potential stealing position, both Flashlight and Pro have shown a willingness to defend or 3-bet. Given the low quality of my cards and the stack depths, folding is the most prudent long-term strategy to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

With Queen-Nine offsuit in the small blind and the button having folded, completing the small blind is a standard and optimal strategy to realize equity against the big blind's range. The hand is strong enough to play post-flop without inflating the pot unnecessarily, and since I am already in for 50 chips, calling for 50 more to complete is the most logical and conservative move.

I have pocket sixes in the Big Blind facing a Small Blind limp. This is a moderately strong hand heads-up, and raising for value and protection is the best play. A raise to 3bb (300) punishes the limp, allows me to take the initiative, and leverages my positional advantage post-flop.

Facing a raise from the Big Blind with Queen-Nine offsuit is a clear fold. The hand has poor equity against an opponent's raising range, and calling out of position will lead to difficult post-flop decisions. Preserving my stack and waiting for a more favorable situation is the optimal strategy.

My hole cards are 4-2 offsuit, which are extremely weak and have very poor equity. Since I am at the button and no one has raised yet, there is no reason to invest chips into this hand. Folding is the disciplined choice here to preserve my stack.

I am in the Small Blind with 87o, a marginal unsuited connector. Flashlight folded on the button. While I could attempt a steal or complete the blind, 87o plays poorly out of position post-flop if Flash calls or raises. The safest and most standard play here is to fold and preserve my stack.

85o is a very weak, uncoordinated hand with no straight, flush, or high card potential. From the button facing an unopened pot, it's not worth trying to steal the blinds with such poor equity and playability if called. A simple fold is the most +EV play.

I am in the Small Blind with Ten-Seven offsuit. The Button has folded. Ten-Seven offsuit is a marginal hand and generally doesn't play well out of position, especially against an active Big Blind like Flashlight. To minimize risk and preserve my stack for better starting hands, I will fold.

I am on the button with Jack-Five offsuit. While I have been successful with some steal attempts in previous hands, J5o is a very weak hand to open with, even from the button. Given the deep stacks and the fact that we are only at hand 58 of 150, there is no need to force a play with a sub-optimal hand. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am in the small blind with a weak hand (5c, 4s). Given that the pot is 150 and it only costs 50 to call, the pot odds are 3:1. While the hand is weak, completing the small blind is a standard play to see a cheap flop in a heads-up scenario.

Holding 53o in the Big Blind against a Small Blind limp, the standard play is to check and see a free flop. The hand has very little equity and raising as a bluff here is unnecessary, while folding is impossible since there's no additional bet to call.

The flop is Jd-9h-Qc. My hand (5c-4s) has completely missed this board, offering no equity or potential draws. Betting here would be a low-success-rate bluff into an opponent who likely has at least a piece of this texture. Checking is the standard play to minimize losses with a hand of this strength.

I have 5-high with no draw on a very connected J-9-Q flop. The Small Blind checked, and betting here as a bluff with zero equity is unnecessary and reckless, especially when a check allows me to see the next card for free or give up later.

The board has paired with the Jack of Spades, creating a Jd-9h-Qc-Js texture. I have absolutely no equity with 5c-4s (high card 5). Any bet here would be a low-percentage bluff that is unlikely to force a fold given the paired board.

With 5-high and no draws on a very coordinated, paired board (J-9-Q-J), I have virtually zero equity. Flashlight has checked again, but bluffing here is highly risky as many hands in their limping range connect with this board. The best option is to check and take a free card.

With five-high on a board of Jd-9h-Qc-Js-As, my hand has zero showdown value. Both my opponent and I have checked through every street, suggesting that neither of us has a strong hand. A bluff is unnecessary and risky given the passive nature of the action so far. Checking is the standard play to reach showdown without further investment.

With 5-high on an extremely connected board (A-Q-J-J-9), I have absolutely zero showdown value. However, turning this hand into a bluff in a 2bb limped pot is unnecessary and risky, as Flashlight's checking range likely contains plenty of marginal made hands that will call a bet. Ace-five offsuit is a strong enough hand to open from the button, aiming to steal the blinds and take initiative in the hand while leveraging my position. The sizing of 250 is consistent with my opening range in previous hands.

I hold AKo in the Small Blind facing a Button open to 250. This is a premium hand, and 3-betting is mandatory for value and to take the betting lead. Out of position, a standard 3-bet size is about 3.5x to 4x the original raise. I will raise to 900 to charge the Button and potentially win the pot pre-flop.

Flashlight raised from the button and Pro 3-bet from the small blind. Holding 8s 7c in the big blind, I am facing significant strength from both opponents. After opening to 250, Pro 3-bet to 900. With A5o, I have decent pot odds (650 to call into 1250) and position post-flop. While not a premium hand, it is strong enough to defend in this spot and potentially realize equity if I hit an Ace or better on the flop. Folding here feels too tight given the button advantage.

On a dry J-6-4 rainbow board, my range as the pre-flop 3-bettor holds a significant advantage, containing all the overpairs (QQ-AA) and strong top pairs (AJ). Betting small (around 1/3 pot) allows me to represent this strong range, putting pressure on my opponent's marginal hands like small pocket pairs and worse overcards (AQ/KQ). Even when called, I retain equity with two overcards to top pair.

The opponent (Pro) has placed a continuation bet of 600 into a pot of 2500, which is approximately 25% of the pot. Given the small sizing on this board (6d-Js-4h), it is likely a polarized c-bet that could include overcards, small pairs, or air. My Ace-high hand has some showdown value against those bluffs and I have reasonable pot odds (1:4) to continue. Folding here is too conservative, and I have sufficient stack depth to call and re-evaluate on the turn.

The turn card is a 2 of hearts, which doesn't improve my hand and doesn't change the board texture significantly to scare my opponent's calling range from the flop. Flashlight's calling range on the flop likely includes Jx, medium pocket pairs like 77-TT, and some floats. Betting again would turn my AK into a pure bluff with low fold equity against top pair and only minimal fold equity against stubborn pocket pairs. Checking controls the size of the pot and allows me to realize my equity for free if it checks through, or easily fold if faced with a large bet.

Pro checked on the turn on a board of 6d-Js-4h-2h. My hand, Ah-5c, has Ace-high with a backdoor heart draw. Checking behind is the best option to realize equity, keep the pot controlled, and see a free river card where I might improve to a pair of Aces.

The river brings the Ten of hearts, completing a potential runner-runner flush and pairing some floating broadway combinations. My Ace-King high has virtually no showdown value against the range Flashlight called with on the flop, which mostly consists of Jx, medium pocket pairs, and some floats. Having checked the turn, trying to bluff this river would be costly and unconvincing, as many of his one-pair hands or newly made flushes/pairs will call. The most prudent decision is to check and concede the pot if faced with a bet.

I have Ace-high on a board of 6d-Js-4h-2h-Th. The opponent (Pro) has checked twice on the turn and river after c-betting the flop. My hand has showdown value against total air (like K-high or Q-high), but it loses to any pair, which is a significant part of the opponent's range given their 3-bet pre-flop. Betting as a bluff is unlikely to be effective against a range that includes Jacks or Tens, which the opponent will not fold. Checking behind is the most prudent play to take the showdown and potentially win against hands that were also checking down with air.
Q8o is a marginal, weak offsuit hand. As the first to act on the Button, I don't have enough equity to open-raise or call with this hand. It's best to fold and wait for better starting hands.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Trey offsuit. The Button has folded, making this a blind-vs-blind battle. Ace-high is a strong starting hand in this scenario. I will raise to 300 to exert pressure on the Big Blind and potentially take the pot down immediately, or build a pot with a hand that likely has the best equity pre-flop.

Ten-Six offsuit is a weak holding for defending the big blind against a raise from the small blind. While the price is tempting, the hand is easily dominated and playing it out of position against an aggressive opponent is likely to lead to negative expected value. Folding is the disciplined choice to preserve my stack.

I am on the button with 7h 3h. While suited hands have some value in 3-handed play, 73s is at the very bottom of the opening range. I have been active in recent hands, and with the chip leader in the big blind, I prefer to fold this marginal hand to maintain a balanced image and avoid unnecessary post-flop complications with a weak holding.

With 3d-8h, my hand is weak, but I am in the small blind and the button has folded. The cost to complete the big blind is small (50 chips), giving me decent pot odds to see a flop heads-up. I will call to complete the blind.
With T5o in the Big Blind facing a limp from the Small Blind, there is no need to aggressively build the pot. Checking allows us to see a free flop in position.

My hand (3d-8h) is essentially air on this 4c-Qh-Th board. I have no made hand and only a weak backdoor heart draw. Betting here serves no purpose as it will not induce folds from better hands and I have no equity to draw to. Checking is the only logical play to see if the opponent (Pro) checks back or bets.

I have middle pair with a weak kicker on a fairly wet flop. Betting here mostly gets called by better hands or strong draws, and it exposes me to a check-raise. Checking behind allows me to control the pot size and realize my equity.

I am holding 3d-8h on a board of 4c-Qh-Th-As. I have no pair and no real draw, making my hand pure air. Since the Big Blind checked the flop and has checked again on the turn, betting here would be a low-success bluff. The Ace on the turn is likely to have improved or solidified the opponent's range. Holding Q9s in the Big Blind facing a Button open and a Small Blind call, we are getting excellent pot odds (200 to win 700) to see a flop. We missed the flop completely, holding only Queen-high and no direct draws on a Jack-high board with a club flush draw present. We missed the flop entirely, holding only Queen-high with no direct draws on a Jack-high board that includes a flush draw. Facing a continuation bet from the pre-flop raiser, we have virtually no equity to justify calling. Folding is the only sensible option.

I am on the button with Ace-Five offsuit, which is a solid hand to play from this position. With only the blinds in the pot, raising to 300 is a standard move to take control, potentially steal the blinds, or play the hand in a favorable position if called.

King-Ten offsuit is a marginal hand that plays poorly out of position. Facing a 3bb open from the Button, calling from the Small Blind often leads to dominated situations or tough post-flop decisions out of position. Folding is the standard and safest play here.

I am in the Big Blind with Ten-Three suited. The Button raised to 3 big blinds and the Small Blind called. I hold pocket deuces on a flop of Queen-King-Four. I am holding 7-2 offsuit, which is statistically the worst starting hand in Texas Hold'em. With no reason to commit chips to a pot with such a weak holding, folding is the only logical decision.

I have flopped top pair with a ten kicker on a relatively low, coordinated board (9-7-5). In a three-handed game, top pair is a strong hand against a button opening range. I am in the small blind with Ace-high on a King-Queen-2-3 board. As the button with a suited King (Kc, 7c), I have a strong positional advantage. Raising to 300 is a standard opening size to steal the blinds or gain the initiative, as my hand is playable in a 3-handed game.

J9o is a marginal offsuit hand that plays poorly out of position. Facing a 3bb raise from the Button, calling from the Small Blind is unprofitable, especially with the Big Blind still yet to act behind me. Folding is the standard and most optimal choice here to preserve chips.

On the turn, the 2nd of hearts is a total brick and does not change the board state.
